profile.summary: Owns product direction across multiple teams or a whole product area, balancing strategy, delivery, and people leadership. Sets priorities, shapes roadmap decisions, and aligns product work with company goals. Usually works closely with engineering, design, data, go-to-market leaders, and executives while coaching product managers and handling trade-offs across short-term delivery and longer-term bets.

known_challenges.text: Balancing strategic product bets with delivery pressure from the business and leadership team.
Making clear prioritisation calls when many teams and stakeholders want different outcomes.
Carrying decision load across roadmap, people, and executive alignment without enough quiet thinking time.
Developing product managers while still stepping in on high-risk decisions and escalations.
Keeping teams outcome-focused instead of slipping into feature output and reactive work.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role is expected to turn broad company goals into clear product choices and explain what will not be done.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Team health and product manager growth
recommended_question_themes.why: A big part of success is building stronger PMs and making sure leadership load is not concentrated in one person.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Stakeholder alignment with executives and peers
recommended_question_themes.why: Misalignment at this level creates churn, weak prioritisation, and constant roadmap resets.
This role often absorbs ambiguity and pressure from many directions, which can quietly drain energy and judgment.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Product operating model and ways of working
recommended_question_themes.why: The quality of planning, discovery, and cross-functional collaboration shapes how well the whole product group performs.

terminology.meaning: The choices about where to play and how to win.
A view of priorities, timing, and major product bets.
The user or business change a team aims to create.
Work to understand problems before committing to solutions.
Evidence that a product solves a real market need.
A key measure of product value over time.
The set of products or product areas under oversight.
Choosing what gets focus and what does not.
Shared understanding with senior leaders on direction and trade-offs.
How product teams plan, decide, and work together.
How teams and responsibilities are structured.
Helping product managers improve judgment and execution.
Product, design, and engineering leading a team together.
Regular learning from users alongside delivery work.
Goals and measurable results used to align teams.
How a product launch reaches and supports customers.
A forum to assess progress, risks, and decisions.
An issue raised upward because teams cannot resolve it alone.

listen_for: Whether they talk in outcomes and trade-offs or mainly in feature lists
How they describe product managers: growing leaders or people needing rescue
Frequent references to stakeholder churn, late changes, or unclear decisions
Whether they can connect roadmap choices to company goals and customer evidence
Signs they are the bottleneck for decisions, approvals, or escalation handling
avoid: Treating them like a senior individual contributor instead of a leader of product direction and people
Pushing for more roadmap certainty when the real issue is unresolved strategy or alignment
Assuming delivery speed alone is the main measure of success
Undermining their product managers by routing every important decision directly through them
